question: What is Spring and Spring Boot? What are the advantages of Spring Boot?
answer: Spring Framework is a comprehensive Java application framework providing dependency injection (IoC), AOP, transaction management, MVC, security, and integration abstractions. It reduced Java EE boilerplate but required significant XML or Java configuration. Spring Boot is an opinionated framework built on top of Spring that makes production-ready applications with minimal configuration. It follows 'convention over configuration'. Key advantages: (1) Auto-configuration — Spring Boot examines the classpath and automatically configures beans. If spring-boot-starter-data-jpa is on the classpath, it auto-configures DataSource, EntityManagerFactory, and TransactionManager. (2) Embedded servers — bundles Tomcat, Jetty, or Undertow. Run as 'java -jar app.jar' without a separate server installation. (3) Starters — curated dependency sets with compatible versions. spring-boot-starter-web pulls Spring MVC, Jackson, Tomcat with aligned versions. No more dependency version conflicts. (4) Actuator — production monitoring endpoints (health, metrics, env) out of the box. (5) Spring Initializr — project scaffolding at start.spring.io. (6) No XML — everything configured in Java or YAML. (7) Production-ready defaults — sensible defaults for connection pools, thread pools, caching.

question: What are Spring Boot starters?
answer: Spring Boot starters are pre-packaged dependency descriptors that bring together all the libraries needed for a particular feature set. Before starters, you had to manually find compatible versions of every library you needed. A single starter dependency transitively brings in all required dependencies with tested, compatible versions. Spring Boot BOM (Bill of Materials) manages all version numbers centrally. Common starters: spring-boot-starter-web (Spring MVC + Tomcat + Jackson), spring-boot-starter-data-jpa (JPA + Hibernate + HikariCP + Spring Data), spring-boot-starter-security (Spring Security), spring-boot-starter-test (JUnit 5 + Mockito + AssertJ + MockMvc), spring-boot-starter-actuator (Actuator endpoints), spring-boot-starter-kafka (Spring Kafka). You can create custom starters for company-wide shared functionality — package your auto-configuration class, list it in META-INF/spring/AutoConfiguration.imports, and publish as a Maven artifact. Teams use custom starters for standardized logging, security config, or observability across all microservices.
codeExample:
<!-- pom.xml — starters manage all transitive dependencies -->
<parent>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-parent</artifactId>
    <version>3.2.0</version>
</parent>

<dependencies>
    <!-- Web: Spring MVC + Jackson + Embedded Tomcat -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
    </dependency>
    <!-- JPA: Hibernate + HikariCP + Spring Data JPA -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-data-jpa</artifactId>
    </dependency>
    <!-- Testing: JUnit5 + Mockito + AssertJ + MockMvc -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-test</artifactId>
        <scope>test</scope>
    </dependency>
</dependencies>

// Custom starter — company standard logging
// 1. auto-configuration class
@AutoConfiguration
@ConditionalOnMissingBean(LoggingService.class)
public class CompanyLoggingAutoConfig {
    @Bean
    public LoggingService loggingService() {
        return new CompanyLoggingService();
    }
}
// 2. META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports
// com.company.starter.CompanyLoggingAutoConfig

question: What is the default port number of a Spring Boot application?
answer: Spring Boot applications run on port 8080 by default when using the embedded Tomcat, Jetty, or Undertow server. This default is defined in the ServerProperties class. To change it: set server.port in application.properties or application.yml. Setting server.port=0 causes Spring Boot to use a random available port — useful for integration tests to avoid port conflicts when multiple tests run in parallel. The actual assigned port can be retrieved from the Environment or by listening to the WebServerInitializedEvent. For management/actuator endpoints, a separate port can be configured with management.server.port. If that is not set, actuator endpoints share the main server port.

question: What is IoC (Inversion of Control)?
answer: Inversion of Control is a design principle where the control of object creation and lifecycle is inverted — instead of objects creating their own dependencies, a framework (the IoC container) creates and manages objects and injects their dependencies. Without IoC, a class creates its own dependencies with 'new', tightly coupling it to specific implementations. With IoC, the class declares what it needs (through constructor parameters, setter methods, or field annotations), and the container provides the implementations. This makes code more modular, testable, and maintainable. In Spring, the IoC container is represented by the ApplicationContext interface. It reads bean definitions (from annotations, XML, or Java config), instantiates objects, resolves dependencies, and manages the full lifecycle. The container is responsible for: (1) Creating bean instances, (2) Wiring dependencies, (3) Calling lifecycle callbacks (@PostConstruct, @PreDestroy), (4) Managing bean scope (singleton, prototype, request, session). The main benefits: loose coupling (programming to interfaces), testability (inject mocks), and centralized lifecycle management.

question: What is Dependency Injection (DI) and what are the types of DI?
answer: Dependency Injection is the mechanism that implements IoC — the container injects required dependencies into objects rather than objects creating them. There are three types: (1) Constructor injection — dependencies provided through the constructor. This is the preferred approach: dependencies are required (cannot create object without them), field is final (immutable after construction), easier to test (just call constructor), and makes circular dependencies immediately visible at startup. (2) Setter injection — dependencies provided through setter methods after object construction. Use for optional dependencies. Can be reconfigured after construction but makes the object mutable. (3) Field injection — dependencies injected directly into fields via @Autowired annotation without constructor or setter. Concise but has significant drawbacks: fields cannot be final, difficult to write unit tests without Spring container, circular dependencies fail silently at runtime, makes mandatory dependencies look optional. Spring recommends constructor injection as best practice. In Spring Boot, if a class has a single constructor, @Autowired is optional — Spring automatically uses it for injection.

question: What is Autowiring? What are the different Autowiring modes?
answer: @Autowired tells Spring to inject a dependency automatically. Spring resolves the bean to inject by type (byType) first — it finds all beans of the matching type in the ApplicationContext. Autowiring modes (relevant in annotation-based Spring): (1) byType (default with @Autowired) — Spring finds a bean matching the declared type. If zero beans match: throws NoSuchBeanDefinitionException (unless required=false). If multiple beans match: throws NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException — resolved with @Qualifier or @Primary. (2) byName — Spring matches the bean name to the field or parameter name. With @Autowired, if multiple beans of the same type exist, Spring falls back to name matching using the field name. (3) @Qualifier — explicitly specify which bean to inject by name when multiple candidates exist. (4) @Primary — mark one bean as the default candidate when multiple exist. (5) constructor — autowiring through constructor (recommended). Legacy XML-based autowiring modes: no (default), byName, byType, constructor, autodetect.

question: What is a Spring Bean, life cycle of Spring Bean, and scopes of Bean?
answer: A Spring Bean is any object managed by the Spring IoC container. The container creates, configures, and destroys beans. Bean lifecycle: (1) Bean definition loading — @ComponentScan, @Configuration, or XML reads bean metadata. (2) BeanFactoryPostProcessors run — modify bean definitions before instantiation. (3) Instantiation — container creates the bean instance (via constructor). (4) Dependency injection — @Autowired, @Value, setter injection. (5) Aware interfaces — BeanNameAware.setBeanName(), ApplicationContextAware.setApplicationContext() called. (6) BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization() — AOP proxies, @PostConstruct processing. (7) @PostConstruct method called. (8) InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet() called. (9) Custom init-method called. (10) BeanPostProcessor.postProcessAfterInitialization() — finalize AOP proxies. (11) Bean is ready for use. (12) On shutdown: @PreDestroy called, DisposableBean.destroy() called, custom destroy-method called. Bean scopes: singleton (one instance per ApplicationContext — default), prototype (new instance every request), request (one per HTTP request), session (one per HTTP session), application (one per ServletContext), websocket.

question: What is a REST API?
answer: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing networked applications. A REST API uses HTTP as its protocol and treats every piece of data or functionality as a resource identified by a URL. Roy Fielding defined REST in his 2000 dissertation with six constraints: (1) Client-Server — separation of concerns between UI and data storage. (2) Stateless — each request contains all information needed; no session state on the server. (3) Cacheable — responses must define themselves as cacheable or non-cacheable. (4) Uniform Interface — standardized way to interact with resources: resource identification in requests (URL), resource manipulation through representations, self-descriptive messages, HATEOAS. (5) Layered System — client cannot tell if it's connected directly to the end server or an intermediary. (6) Code on Demand (optional) — server can extend client functionality by sending executable code. In practice, REST APIs use HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE), return JSON or XML representations of resources, use HTTP status codes for response classification, and are typically versioned in the URL (/api/v1/) or via headers. Most 'REST' APIs in practice are REST-like (RESTful) rather than strictly adhering to all Fielding constraints, particularly HATEOAS.

question: What are the HTTP methods used in REST API?
answer: HTTP defines a set of request methods (verbs) indicating the desired action. In REST APIs: GET — retrieve a resource or collection. Must be safe (no side effects) and idempotent (same result if called multiple times). Responses can be cached. POST — create a new resource. Not idempotent — calling twice creates two resources. Not safe. Returns 201 Created with Location header. PUT — replace an entire resource. Idempotent — calling PUT twice with same data produces same result. If resource doesn't exist, creates it (some implementations). PATCH — partial update of a resource. Not necessarily idempotent depending on implementation (e.g., 'increment by 1' is not idempotent). DELETE — remove a resource. Idempotent — deleting an already-deleted resource returns 404 or 204. HEAD — same as GET but returns only headers, no body. Used to check resource existence or get metadata. OPTIONS — returns allowed methods for a resource. Used in CORS preflight requests. HTTP status code families: 2xx Success (200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content), 3xx Redirection (301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified), 4xx Client Error (400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict, 422 Unprocessable Entity), 5xx Server Error (500 Internal Server Error, 503 Service Unavailable).

question: What is JSON and XML?
answer: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) and XML (eXtensible Markup Language) are two data interchange formats used in REST APIs and data storage. JSON: lightweight, human-readable text format. Supports: objects ({key: value}), arrays ([value1, value2]), strings, numbers, booleans, null. No support for comments, no schema in the format itself (use JSON Schema separately). Native to JavaScript; all modern languages have JSON parsers. Smaller payload than XML (no closing tags). Parsed faster than XML. Default format for Spring Boot REST APIs via Jackson. XML: verbose but expressive markup language. Supports attributes, namespaces, CDATA sections, entity references. Has built-in schema validation (XSD), transformation (XSLT), and querying (XPath/XQuery). Required for SOAP web services. Still common in enterprise, financial, and government systems. More self-documenting but larger payload. In Spring Boot: Jackson (ObjectMapper) handles JSON serialization/deserialization automatically. For XML, Jackson-dataformat-xml or JAXB is used. Content negotiation via Accept header determines which format is returned.

question: What is JWT (JSON Web Token) and OAuth?
answer: JWT (JSON Web Token) is a compact, URL-safe token format for representing claims between parties. A JWT consists of three Base64URL-encoded parts separated by dots: Header.Payload.Signature. Header: algorithm and token type (HS256, RS256). Payload: claims — standard (iss, sub, exp, iat, jti) and custom (userId, roles). Signature: HMAC or RSA signature verifying the token hasn't been tampered with. JWTs are self-contained — the server doesn't need to query a database to validate a token; it verifies the signature and checks expiry. Stateless authentication. OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework (not authentication) that allows third-party applications to access resources on behalf of a user without exposing credentials. OAuth 2.0 defines four grant types: Authorization Code (web apps — safest), Client Credentials (machine-to-machine), Resource Owner Password (legacy — not recommended), Implicit (deprecated). OpenID Connect (OIDC) is built on top of OAuth 2.0 to add identity (authentication). OAuth gives you an access token (often JWT); OIDC additionally gives an ID token (always JWT) containing user identity. In Spring Security, JWTs are validated using spring-boot-starter-oauth2-resource-server for services that receive JWTs, and spring-boot-starter-oauth2-authorization-server for issuing them.

question: What is Spring Security?
answer: Spring Security is a powerful and highly customizable authentication and access-control framework for Java applications. It integrates with Spring Boot through auto-configuration — simply adding spring-boot-starter-security enables: HTTP Basic authentication, form-based login, CSRF protection, session management, and security headers (X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, etc.). Core concepts: (1) Authentication — verifying who you are. SecurityContext holds the Authentication object for the current thread/request. (2) Authorization — what you're allowed to do. Method security (@PreAuthorize, @Secured) and URL-pattern security. (3) Filter chain — Spring Security operates as a chain of servlet filters. Each filter handles one concern: UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter (form login), BearerTokenAuthenticationFilter (JWT), ExceptionTranslationFilter (401/403 handling). (4) UserDetailsService — Spring Security interface for loading user information from any source (database, LDAP, in-memory). (5) PasswordEncoder — secure password hashing. BCryptPasswordEncoder is the standard. In Spring Boot 3+, the security configuration uses the SecurityFilterChain bean approach (replacing the deprecated WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter).

question: How do you secure REST APIs in Spring Boot?
answer: Securing REST APIs requires multiple layers. Authentication layer: use JWT tokens issued by an authorization server (Keycloak, Auth0, or Spring Authorization Server). Every API request must include a Bearer token in the Authorization header. The resource server validates the JWT signature, expiry, and audience without a database call. Authorization layer: role-based access control (RBAC) using @PreAuthorize with SpEL expressions or URL-pattern security in SecurityFilterChain. For fine-grained control, use Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) where authorization depends on resource attributes. Transport security: always HTTPS. Never expose APIs over plain HTTP in production. Use HSTS header. Input validation: use Bean Validation (@Valid, @NotNull, @Size) on all request bodies and parameters. Validate at controller level, not service level. Rate limiting: prevent abuse with RequestRateLimiter in Spring Cloud Gateway or Bucket4j for per-service limiting. CSRF protection: disable for stateless REST APIs (no session-based CSRF attack is possible). Enable for form-based logins. Security headers: Spring Security adds X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, X-XSS-Protection automatically. Add Content-Security-Policy manually.

question: What is Microservices architecture and Monolithic architecture?
answer: Monolithic architecture: the entire application is built as a single deployable unit. All components — UI, business logic, data access — are in one codebase, compiled together, and deployed together as one process. Simple to develop initially, easy to test end-to-end, low operational overhead. Problems at scale: a small change requires redeploying the entire application (risk + slow); scaling means replicating everything (even components that don't need scaling); teams step on each other's code; technology choices are locked; one bug can crash the entire system. Microservices architecture: decompose the application into small, independently deployable services. Each service: owns its bounded context (a specific business capability), owns its data (database per service), is independently deployable, communicates via REST or messaging, can be scaled independently, can use its own technology stack. Benefits: independent deployment, independent scaling, team autonomy, technology diversity, fault isolation. Challenges: distributed systems complexity (network failures, distributed transactions, eventual consistency), operational complexity (many services to deploy, monitor, and trace), service discovery, API versioning. Conway's Law: organizations design systems matching their communication structure. Microservices require cross-functional teams organized around services.

question: How do microservices communicate with each other?
answer: Microservices communicate through two main patterns. Synchronous communication: (1) REST over HTTP — most common. Simple, widely understood. Uses Feign Client (declarative HTTP client) or WebClient (reactive). Drawbacks: tight temporal coupling — if service B is down, service A's request fails. (2) gRPC — protocol buffers + HTTP/2. Binary protocol, faster than JSON/REST, strongly typed contracts. Used for internal service-to-service communication where performance matters. Asynchronous communication: (3) Message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ) — services publish events to topics/queues. Consumers process when ready. Temporally decoupled — producer doesn't wait for consumer. Enables event-driven architecture. Higher resilience. Complexity: eventual consistency, message ordering, idempotency. Service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) adds communication features transparently: mTLS, retries, circuit breaking, load balancing without code changes. When to use which: use REST for queries where you need an immediate response; use Kafka/messaging for state-changing commands where you can tolerate eventual consistency; use gRPC for high-throughput internal calls where performance is critical.

question: What is an API Gateway?
answer: An API Gateway is the single entry point for all external requests to a microservices system. It acts as a reverse proxy, routing requests to appropriate backend services. Without it, clients would need to know the address of every service and handle cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting) individually. Core responsibilities: (1) Request routing — map /api/orders/** to order-service, /api/products/** to product-service. (2) Authentication — validate JWT tokens once at the gateway. Downstream services receive enriched headers (X-User-Id, X-Roles) and can trust them without re-validating. (3) Rate limiting — control request rates per client IP or API key using Redis sliding window. (4) Load balancing — distribute traffic across multiple instances of the same service using round-robin or least-connections. (5) SSL termination — handle HTTPS externally, communicate internally over HTTP. (6) Request transformation — add/remove headers, rewrite paths, transform payloads. (7) Circuit breaking — fail fast when a downstream service is unhealthy. (8) Caching — cache GET responses to reduce backend load. (9) Observability — centralized access logging, metrics, distributed trace initiation. In Spring Boot: Spring Cloud Gateway (WebFlux-based, non-blocking) is the recommended solution.
codeExample:
// Spring Cloud Gateway as API Gateway
@SpringBootApplication
public class ApiGatewayApp { ... }

spring:
  cloud:
    gateway:
      default-filters:
        - DedupeResponseHeader=Access-Control-Allow-Credentials,
              Access-Control-Allow-Origin
      globalcors:
        cors-configurations:
          '[/**]':
            allowedOrigins: "*"
            allowedMethods: "*"
            allowedHeaders: "*"
      routes:
        # Order service
        - id: order-service
          uri: lb://ORDER-SERVICE      # Eureka-based LB
          predicates:
            - Path=/api/orders/**
          filters:
            - StripPrefix=1
            - name: CircuitBreaker
              args:
                name: orderCB
                fallbackUri: forward:/fallback
            - name: RequestRateLimiter
              args:
                key-resolver: "#{@userIdKeyResolver}"
                redis-rate-limiter.replenishRate: 100
                redis-rate-limiter.burstCapacity: 200

@Component
public class UserIdKeyResolver implements KeyResolver {
    @Override
    public Mono<String> resolve(ServerWebExchange exchange) {
        // Rate limit per user from JWT
        return Mono.just(
            exchange.getRequest()
                .getHeaders()
                .getFirst("X-User-Id"));
    }
}

question: What is Spring Data JPA?
answer: Spring Data JPA is a layer on top of JPA (Java Persistence API) and Hibernate that dramatically reduces boilerplate data access code. Without Spring Data, you write EntityManager calls for every CRUD operation. Spring Data JPA provides: (1) Repository interfaces — extend JpaRepository<Entity, ID> and get save(), findById(), findAll(), delete(), count() and many more methods for free without writing any code. (2) Query derivation — method name conventions are automatically converted to JPQL: findByEmailAndActive(String email, boolean active) becomes SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.email = :email AND u.active = :active. (3) @Query — custom JPQL or native SQL for complex queries. (4) Pagination and sorting — Pageable parameter and Page return type for automatic pagination with total count. (5) Specifications — type-safe dynamic queries using JPA Criteria API. (6) Auditing — @CreatedDate, @LastModifiedDate automatically populated using @EnableJpaAuditing. (7) Projections — interfaces or DTOs for selecting specific columns. (8) Spring Data REST — automatically exposes repositories as REST endpoints (use carefully in production). The key insight: Spring Data generates the implementation of your repository interface at runtime using byte manipulation.

question: What is a Repository? What are the types of Repositories in Spring Data?
answer: A Repository in Spring Data is a pattern that abstracts data access logic into an interface. You declare the interface with method signatures; Spring Data generates the implementation. The repository hierarchy from most basic to most feature-rich: (1) Repository<T, ID> — marker interface. No methods. Derive your own methods via query derivation. (2) CrudRepository<T, ID> — adds save(), findById(), findAll(), deleteById(), count(), existsById(). (3) PagingAndSortingRepository<T, ID> — extends CrudRepository, adds findAll(Pageable) and findAll(Sort) for pagination and sorting. (4) JpaRepository<T, ID> — extends PagingAndSortingRepository with JPA-specific methods: flush(), saveAndFlush(), deleteInBatch(), findAll(Example<T>) (Query by Example), getReferenceById() (lazy proxy). Most commonly used. (5) JpaSpecificationExecutor<T> — additional interface providing Specification-based querying (dynamic criteria). (6) QuerydslPredicateExecutor<T> — Querydsl integration for type-safe dynamic queries. (7) ReactiveCrudRepository<T, ID> — for reactive (WebFlux/R2DBC) applications, returns Mono/Flux. (8) Custom repository implementations — implement a custom interface and name the class MyRepositoryImpl to add it to the generated repository.

question: How do you handle exceptions in Spring Boot?
answer: Spring Boot provides several levels of exception handling. (1) @ExceptionHandler on a single controller — handles exceptions thrown only by that controller. Limited scope. (2) @RestControllerAdvice (or @ControllerAdvice + @ResponseBody) — global exception handler. Catches exceptions from all controllers. The recommended approach for REST APIs. Map specific exceptions to appropriate HTTP status codes with meaningful error bodies. (3) ResponseStatusException — throw directly from controllers/services with an HTTP status code without needing a separate handler method. (4) ProblemDetail (Spring Boot 3+ / RFC 7807) — standardized error response format with type, title, status, detail, instance fields. Enable with spring.mvc.problemdetails.enabled=true. (5) ErrorController — for errors that occur in filters (before reaching controllers), implement ErrorController to handle /error mapping. Key principles: never expose internal exception messages (stack traces) to clients; always return structured error responses; log the full exception server-side; use appropriate HTTP status codes (400 for validation, 401 for auth, 404 for not found, 409 for conflict, 500 for unexpected).

question: What is application.properties or application.yml?
answer: application.properties and application.yml are the primary externalized configuration files in Spring Boot. They store configuration values that the application reads at startup. Spring Boot reads them from multiple locations in precedence order (higher overrides lower): classpath:/, classpath:/config/, file:./, file:./config/. application.properties uses key=value format; application.yml uses YAML's hierarchical structure which is more readable for nested properties. Both are loaded automatically by Spring Boot's PropertySourcesLoader — no explicit annotation needed. Key uses: server configuration (port, SSL), datasource configuration (URL, credentials), Spring profiles activation, logging levels, feature flags, any custom application properties. Profile-specific files (application-dev.yml, application-prod.yml) activate additional properties for specific profiles, overriding base file values. In Spring Boot 2.4+, config files can import other files with spring.config.import=optional:file:/etc/secrets.properties allowing secrets to be mounted from external sources. Environment variables automatically override any property using relaxed binding: MY_DB_PASSWORD overrides spring.datasource.password.
codeExample:
# application.properties format
server.port=8080
server.servlet.context-path=/api

spring.datasource.url=jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/mydb
spring.datasource.username=dbuser
spring.datasource.password=${DB_PASSWORD} # env var interpolation
spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=20

spring.jpa.show-sql=false
spring.jpa.open-in-view=false

logging.level.com.example=INFO
logging.level.org.hibernate.SQL=DEBUG

# Custom properties
app.max-upload-size-mb=10
app.jwt.secret=${JWT_SECRET}
app.jwt.expiry-seconds=3600

---
# Equivalent application.yml format
server:
  port: 8080
  servlet:
    context-path: /api

spring:
  datasource:
    url: jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/mydb
    username: dbuser
    password: ${DB_PASSWORD}
    hikari:
      maximum-pool-size: 20
  jpa:
    show-sql: false
    open-in-view: false

logging:
  level:
    com.example: INFO
    org.hibernate.SQL: DEBUG

app:
  max-upload-size-mb: 10
  jwt:
    secret: ${JWT_SECRET}
    expiry-seconds: 3600
